title: Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Net](#dod:net-oda) [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita

Net official development assistance (ODA), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

This scatter plot visualizes the relationship between GDP per capita and net official development assistance (ODA) given per capita for a selection of countries. The horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in 2021 international dollars, ranging from $0 to $120,000, while the vertical axis shows net ODA given per capita in constant 2023 US dollars, spanning from $0 to over $800. Data points are color-coded by region: North America in orange, Europe in blue, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue. Circle size corresponds to population, with a reference scale indicating a 300 million population marker. Norway sits at the highest point on the vertical axis, exceeding $800 in aid per capita, positioned near the $90,000 mark on the horizontal axis. Luxembourg and Ireland appear in the upper right quadrant, with GDP per capita values approaching or exceeding $110,000 and aid levels between $400 and $500. Switzerland and Sweden cluster in the mid-to-upper range of the aid axis, between $400 and $500, with GDP per capita figures around $60,000 to $70,000. The United States is represented by a large orange circle, indicating a high population, positioned near $80,000 GDP per capita and roughly $200 in aid per capita. Qatar appears as a distinct outlier on the far right with a GDP per capita over $110,000 but lower aid contributions around $250. A dense cluster of Asian and European nations, including Japan, South Korea, Turkey, and the United Kingdom, occupies the lower-left to central area of the chart, generally showing GDP per capita between $30,000 and $60,000 and aid levels below $250. Thailand and Kazakhstan sit at the lower end of both axes, with GDP per capita near $20,000 to $30,000 and minimal aid contributions.
